danger_signals.visual_form: Distinct enemy character models with thematic designs per continent
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate combat threat; hostile entities in environment

danger_signals.visual_form: Four unique boss entity designs representing Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse
danger_signals.meaning: Major world-state threat; progression gates; faction leadership presence

danger_signals.visual_form: Visually distinct supreme antagonist entity housed in Cathedral
danger_signals.meaning: World-enslaving threat; ultimate danger; world-state dependent on defeat

safety_signals.visual_form: Traversable level connections showing pathways between safe zones
safety_signals.meaning: Navigable safe passage; progression availability; spatial control

safety_signals.visual_form: Recurring artifact item with distinctive appearance
safety_signals.meaning: Power increase; temporary invulnerability or enhanced capability; resource safety

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Serpent iconography (implied by faction name and Four Horsemen reference)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Three Serpent Rider brothers (previous games); Eidolon (oldest, most powerful); four generals under Eidolon command
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Cathedral as Eidolon's dominion; enslaved world Thyrion; continent-based strongholds

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Four playable character classes (Paladin, Crusader, Necromancer, Assassin) with unique visual designs and weapon sets
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Progression through four continents; hub system showing reclamation of territory

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Evil wizard commanding new enemy types in expansion
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Fifth continent Tulku; Sino-Tibetan aesthetic setting; resurrection ritual locations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Four distinct continental environments under hostile control; each continent themed to oppressive regime (Medieval European Blackmarsh, Mesoamerican Mazaera, Ancient Egyptian Thysis, Greco-Roman Septimus)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies patrol and defend faction territories; generals positioned as regional commanders; hub-connected levels show interconnected control structure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Movement from continent to continent; changes in one level affect another (hub system), showing environmental responsiveness to player actions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies adjust positioning; access to new areas unlocks; defeated generals reduce Serpent Rider presence

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character statistics increase; HP and mana pools expand visually in interface
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player character grows in offensive/defensive capability; level-up abilities unlock unique powers per class

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Fifth continent Tulku environment; ritual sites with particle effects (snow); new enemy types appear
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Expansion enemies behave differently; resurrection mechanics alter threat level

environmental_storytelling.example: Four continents each possess distinct architectural and cultural themes (Medieval, Mesoamerican, Egyptian, Greco-Roman), visually communicating different regional administrations under Serpent Rider rule
Level design where changes in one connected level affect another demonstrates integrated world-state; players see consequences of actions propagate through environment
Four generals positioned on separate continents; Eidolon in central Cathedral communicates chain of command and faction structure visually through geography
Environmental destruction capability allows players to alter spaces, visually marking completion and control of areas
Catapults and ballistae in level design (referenced in Siege mode) communicate defensive positions and faction occupation intensity

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Boss-level threat behavior; positioned at continental strongholds; defend faction territories
Isolated in Cathedral; only confronted after all four generals defeated; movement restricted to dominion

Paladin exhibits offensive capability; Crusader exhibits defensive capability; Necromancer casts spells; Assassin exhibits stealth—visual weapon and ability differences communicate playstyle differences
Commands new enemy variants; controls fifth continent; ritual behavior indicates resurrection mechanics in progress

notes: Hexen II uses continental theming and hub-based level interconnection as primary world-state communication mechanisms. Faction identity is communicated through boss positioning, enemy type distribution, and environmental theming rather than explicit color/symbol systems. The game's visual communication relies on environmental storytelling through architecture, geography, and obstacle placement. Character class selection creates visual faction identity for player (Paladin vs. Necromancer have radically different visual presentations). Four Horsemen reference for Serpent Rider generals suggests apocalyptic threat framing of antagonist faction. Hub system with propagating changes indicates state-dependent environment behavior.
sources_used: wikipedia: Hexen II (gameplay, plot, development, expansion sections)
